nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater lagoons, ponds, slow-moving streams, lakes, and floodplains in tropical America

decorPreferences: open background space for dense grouped plantings

summary: Red Cabomba is a striking but demanding stem plant from tropical Central and South America, known for its finely divided, wine-red to deep purple foliage. It requires intense lighting, pressurised CO2, and very stable water conditions — making it one of the more challenging plants in the hobby.
careNotes:
Red Cabomba (Cabomba furcata, also sold under the older name Cabomba piauhyensis) is a perennial stem plant native to tropical Central and South America. Its fan-shaped submerged leaves are finely divided — branching repeatedly into slender segments — and range in colour from olive green through brown-red to deep wine-red or purple under intense lighting and iron supplementation. This is the demanding red species of the genus, distinct from the related Cabomba aquatica (Giant or Yellow Cabomba) and Cabomba caroliniana (Green Cabomba or Carolina Fanwort), each of which has its own entry. Care difficulty is generally considered advanced, with sources consistently rating it as substantially more demanding than the green Cabombas.

High to intense lighting — commonly cited as roughly 30–50 PAR at the substrate, or around 3 W/gal of full-spectrum light — and pressurised CO2 injection are widely advised as effectively mandatory for healthy growth and red coloration. Without them, the plant tends to grow leggy, shed its lower leaves, and eventually collapse. Iron and a comprehensive macro- and micronutrient regime — especially nitrate, phosphate, and chelated iron dosed into the water column — are generally needed to bring out the deepest red-purple tones.

Generally advised to keep at 22–28 °C in soft, slightly acidic water (pH roughly 5.5–7.0, with optimal growth most often reported around pH 6.5; the full range cited across sources spans 5.0–7.5). The plant is reported to be highly sensitive to sudden parameter swings and is prone to melting if temperature, pH, or hardness shift abruptly. Stems are brittle — handle gently when planting or trimming and avoid disturbing established stands. Best appearance is achieved when planted in dense groups in the midground or background, ideally contrasted against lighter green plants.

Note on growth rate: under proper high-light and CO2 conditions, fast growth requiring regular pruning is the general consensus, though rooting and initial establishment can be slow.
breedingNotes: Red Cabomba is propagated by stem cuttings of roughly 4–12 cm. Cut just below a node, gently remove the lowest leaves, and replant the cutting into a nutrient-rich substrate. Lateral side-shoots growing from the base of older stems can also be separated and replanted. Because the stems are brittle, cuttings should be handled with care throughout the process. When grown out of water (emersed), the species can produce small purple flowers.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because Tropica does not list Cabomba furcata in its catalogue (only C. aquatica). Buce Plant confirms: South American origin, family Cabombaceae, difficult care, fast growth, medium-to-high lighting (>=3 W/gal), CO2 injection recommended, pH 5.0-6.8, midground-to-background placement, height ~25 cm, propagation by cutting/splitting/daughter plants, iron fertilisation for red coloration, sensitive to parameter swings.

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Cross-checked: difficult care level, fast growth, 24-30 degC, soft water, strong lighting required, CO2 important, nutrient-rich substrate with full macro/micronutrient supply, 20-60 cm height, midground-to-background placement, propagation by cuttings with new shoots at base of older stems.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 22-28 degC, pH 6.0-7.5, soft to slightly hard (gH 0-8), 30-50 PAR for 8-10 hours, height 30-90 cm under cultivation, native to South America (Brazil, Argentina) and Caribbean, family Cabombaceae, background placement, propagation by stem cuttings, moderate-to-difficult care. Used for averaging the temperature and pH ranges.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-checked: Cabomba furcata prefers 23-26 degC, 6.0-7.5 pH (optimal ~6.5), gH 3-8, intense lighting (~40-50 PAR for red coloration), CO2 recommended, fragile and sensitive to algae, prone to losing lower leaves. Describes rooting/establishment as slow, contrary to the 'fast growth' rating from Buce Plant and Aquasabi - documented in careNotes.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation: family Cabombaceae, order Nymphaeales, synonyms include Cabomba piauhiensis Gardner, Nectris furcata, Cabomba pubescens, Cabomba warmingii. Native range: tropical America (Colombia, Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Venezuela, Costa Rica, Panama, Honduras). Habitat: freshwater lagoons, ponds, streams, lakes, and floodplains.
